So the simplest form of adaptive query optimization
is as I said, where as we execute a query,
we also collect some information about what the data looks like,
and then we can use that information
to decide whether our query is wrong and when we plan it,
or we can then merge that back into the sort of global catalog.
Again, when you think about this,
what if the optimizer actually doing?
You have much of histograms or statistical models
about what your attributes look like.
So for a given predicate in your where clause,
you want to estimate the selectivity of that predicate,
because that'll determine how many tuples your scan will emit
and you can use that to make decisions about
join orderings and other things above in the query plan.
So as you execute the scan,
if you actually know the true selectivity
because you're applying the predicate on the tuples
and you know the number of tuples or percentage
of the tuples are gonna match.
So if you then determine that the cost model estimated
my selectivity was 1%,
but when I run the real query,
and I run the query and actually do the evaluation of the predicate,
my selectivity is 99%,
then I wanted to use that information
to help me decide whether to replay my query
or that future queries come along,
they can exploit the knowledge that I've gained.
Again, the one approach is to try to fix my current query,
or the other one is just merging it back into the overall
statistical models and the catalog
so that I can then help queries in the future.
So the most basic approach to do this
is call a Reversion-Based Plan Correction.
And the idea here, as I said,
is just every single time I invoke a query,
I keep track of what query plan I generated for it,
I keep track of the cost estimations I had for,
and then I'll have all my metrics of what happened
when I ran it, right?
How many tuples I selected,
how much CPU I remember that I used?
And I'm gonna maintain this history
inside the database itself.
So you'll see this in the commercial systems,
like in DB2 Oracle and SQL Server,
they have this built in repository
of the history of every single query
that I ever got them invoked,
and they can use that information to help decide
how to do query planning in the future.
So let's say that we have a perverse statement,
or we have a query that's invoked all the time,
and we have a cache query plan.
So rather than maybe run it through the optimizer
every single time,
we can just use the cache query plan
that we run to generate from previous invocations.
So if now there's a change in the statistics
or something about the database,
the physical design that changes,
and we recognize that we maybe want to,
for this particular query, we keep invoking,
we want to run it back through the optimizer
and see if we generate a new plan.
But then when we run that new plan for this query,
if we see that the performance of the query
is worse than the old plan that we had before,
then we just want to work back to it.
If there's a regression in the performance,
we switch back to the plan that we know
actually performed better for us,
despite the change in the physical design
or the statistical models.
So we use that simple query example that I have a four,
right, the four way join.
Say again, this is my original plan,
and I'm doing nothing but sequential scans and a hash join
and say that my estimated cost is a thousand

so my actual cost actually matches up.

So I'm gonna store in my execution history
for my database system that I generated for this query,
I generated this plan, and when I ran it, I had this cost.

in my database system, right?
You're sort of eating your own dog food,
rather than having an auxiliary store,
this is just another table that you record this information.

in our database design.
Say the DBA comes along and adds to indexes
on the B table and the D table,

this is something that Microsoft has had in SQL Server

oh, this query plan is bad.
Let me just switch back to this one, right?

So the paper you guys were signed reading from Microsoft
is called plan stitching
and the high-level idea is exactly the same
where if we recognize that our query is running slower,
then query plans we saw in the past,
rather than potentially
just throwing away the entire query plan,
the new query plan and reverting back to the old one,
maybe there are elements or aspects or subplans
within the newer plan.
We actually wouldn't wanna retain
because that'll help us lead us towards a better plan,
a more optimal plan, right?
And the other thing you think about
with plan stitch as well is that the subplans
you're gonna borrow from other queries
don't need to be actually from the same query.
Like in this case here,
I can only reuse the plan in the simplest form.
I can only swap a team plans
if they're running on the exact same query,
but with plan stitch,
because I can excise out subplans
or portions of the query plan,
as long as I know that they're logically equivalent,
I can take bits and pieces from other queries, right?
The other interesting thing too is that
if there is a change in the physical design
where a new plan query plan becomes invalid,
meaning like it defined that it wanted to do index scan,
but then I drop that index,
rather than just gonna throw away the entire query,
the query plan and its entirety,
I can maybe again pull out pieces of it, right?
So the basic approach they're gonna use
is the way they're gonna generate these stitch plans
is a dynamic programming search method
using a bottom-up approach
where you just, you check the C from going from one level
to the next and say,
we did a system R,
we're going one node to the next,
you pick which subplan is the best,
and then once you reach the end goal,
you do find the cheapest path.
So this means that it's not guaranteed
to find a better plan than the best plan you have so far,
and it is not guaranteed to always produce a valid plan,
but there's some basic heuristic to use
to make sure that happens.

right, say that this is our new plan

there's still portions that are still usable.

the subplan of this part of the query,
